SemiAnalysis says Meta Superintelligence (MSL) could overtake Google in frontier AI within the next six months after a year of aggressive restructuring, massive capital spending, and infrastructure expansion. The firm argues Meta has built a key competitive advantage through proprietary reinforcement learning data, thousands of engineers dedicated to AI, and the launch of its Muse Spark 1.1 model for developers to compete with OpenAI and Anthropic.  The report expects Meta to surpass OpenAI and Anthropic in total AI compute by year-end as it builds five gigawatt-scale AI data center clusters connected by its custom AI-Backbone network. Reuters also reported Meta plans to invest up to $145 billion in AI infrastructure, deploy 7 GW of compute capacity in 2026 and 14 GW in 2027, while beginning production of its custom "Iris" AI chip developed with Broadcom and manufactured by TSMC.   $META $GOOGL $AVGO $TSM

$AMZN $GS $MRVL $NVDA $AVGO    Goldman raises Amazon 2027-2030 capex estimates for AWS spending    Goldman Sachs analyst Eric Sheridan said ahead of Amazon's upcoming earnings report that the stock has remained volatile intra-quarter, with a downward trend as investor focus has shifted across an array of debates. The firm is raising its 2027-2030 capex estimates for AWS on the back of continued constraints between the demand for AI and the wider supply of compute, telling investors that it now expects Amazon to spend a cumulative $827B between 2026-2028E before capex growth decelerating to a more normalized level in 2029. The reiterated its Buy rating and raised its 12-month price target on Amazon shares to $335 from $325.

Wall Street spent the last few weeks worrying that the AI buildout was getting too expensive. Micron’s response was basically: fine, here’s a $250B investment plan.    That number runs through 2035, so this is not one giant check being written tomorrow. Still, the message is hard to miss. $MU is securing raw silicon supply, expanding U.S. manufacturing and preparing for a memory market that it clearly believes will be much larger than today’s. The funny part is that investors are debating whether AI demand has peaked while the companies closest to the bottlenecks are still planning capacity years ahead.    Of course, big capex does not guarantee big returns. If everyone builds at once, today’s shortage can become tomorrow’s oversupply. But companies do not commit this kind of money because they expect AI infrastructure to disappear next quarter.    So what are we looking at here: smart long-term positioning, or the first seeds of the next semiconductor glut?  $NVDA $AVGO $SOXX

Apple announced that its expanded semiconductor agreement with Broadcom is expected to exceed $30 billion through 2031, reinforcing its commitment to invest $600 billion in the U.S. The partnership will support the production of more than 15 billion chips in the United States and includes $1.5 billion to modernize Broadcom's manufacturing facility in Fort Collins, Colorado, where advanced radio-frequency components for wireless chips will be produced.  The announcement provides new financial details on the supply agreement Broadcom disclosed earlier this week. Broadcom shares climbed as much as 5% following the news. The company has long supplied Apple with wireless connectivity chips and, despite Apple's shift toward designing some of its own components, Broadcom continues to provide key communications technologies. It is also developing chip technology expected to power Apple's first dedicated AI server, slated for launch next year.  $AAPL $AVGO
